Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd mostly just drift through the week and then realize I had something due. When I did study, I'd watch videos or look at diagrams since that's how I learn best, but it was pretty random. I'd maybe do a couple hours here and there when I felt like it, but nothing structured.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then maybe cram a bit if I remembered a deadline was coming up. It really depended on what else was going on in my life or if I was just too bored to do anything else. Regular study times? That's not really my thing.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I got an email reminder or notification about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything new, scroll around for a bit, then leave. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me to log in a few more times because they were less boring than regular assignments.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in a few days in a row, but then I'd completely forget about it for a week or two. The LMS just felt like another thing I was supposed to do but couldn't be bothered with most of the time.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could actually see what was being explained. I'm a visual learner so reading long texts just doesn't work for me. When there were diagrams or visual explanations, I'd actually pay attention and remember stuff better. The gamified tasks were also kind of engaging because they broke up the monotony.
Q6: The discussion forums I basically ignored completely. Reading through other people's posts felt like a waste of time and I never felt like I had anything worth contributing. The long reading materials I'd skip too because they were just walls of text. I'd maybe skim them once but that's about it.
Q7: If something was quick and I could see the point of it, I'd do it. The gamified stuff felt worth my time because it was actually kind of fun and didn't feel like traditional homework. But if something seemed like busywork or took forever with no clear benefit, I'd just skip it or half-do it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff I basically never touched. If it wasn't required, my brain just filed it under 'not my problem.' Required things I'd at least attempt, even if I did them last minute. There's something about knowing it doesn't count that makes it impossible for me to care about it.
Q9: Yeah, I'd download videos or save images and diagrams to my phone and look at them later when I wasn't online. Sometimes I'd just think through concepts while doing other stuff, like walking or whatever. I also used YouTube a lot to find better explanations of things the course material didn't make clear.
Q10: There were times I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay it in my head or sketch out diagrams on paper to understand it better. None of that would show up anywhere. Also, I'd sometimes discuss stuff with a mate who was in the same course, but we'd do that over text, not on the platform.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download stuff and then never go back to the LMS for it. I had a folder on my laptop with videos and images I'd saved. It was easier to access them offline than logging back in every time. So my actual study time was probably more than what the system recorded.
Q12: Pretty much most of the semester felt hard to engage with, honestly. I was just bored a lot of the time. The content didn't grab me and I kept thinking about other things I'd rather be doing. When assignments piled up I'd feel stressed but still couldn't make myself care enough to do them properly.
Q13: Yeah, all of that affected me. I was stressed about money stuff since I don't have a lot of resources, and that made it hard to focus on coursework. Sometimes I'd be too tired to even open the LMS. When things got confusing in the course, I'd just give up instead of trying to figure it out.
Q14: All the time. I'd open videos and let them play while I was on my phone doing something else. Or I'd scroll through materials without really reading them. I was technically 'active' but my brain was somewhere else entirely. It was like going through the motions without actually learning anything.
Q15: Yeah, basically everything. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. I think part of it was that starting felt overwhelming, and part of it was just not caring enough to push through the boredom.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore stuff until the last possible moment and then rush to submit something. Without deadlines I probably wouldn't have done anything at all. They stressed me out but also were the only structure I had.
Q17: Not really. By the time feedback came back I'd already moved on mentally. I'd glance at it but I wouldn't go back and redo things or change my approach. It felt like feedback was always about stuff that was already done, so what was the point of dwelling on it?
Q18: The course structure was pretty loose which honestly didn't help me. I need more external pressure to get things done. When everything was laid out as 'here are your materials, figure it out,' I'd just drift. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they added some structure and made things less boring.
Q19: Having everything be self-paced made it harder because I'd just procrastinate forever. What made it easier was when things were visual and interactive instead of just text. The gamification helped too because it gave me small goals to hit instead of these massive assignments looming over me.
Q20: That could be someone like me who logs in and clicks around but doesn't actually commit to finishing things. You might open a bunch of stuff out of curiosity or boredom but then not follow through. Or maybe they're trying to figure out what's required without actually doing the work yet.
Q21: That's basically me. I didn't use the LMS much but I still got decent exam scores because I'm pretty good at cramming and I studied in ways that weren't tracked. I'd use outside resources and study offline. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything that goes into actually learning the material.
Q22: It can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. It misses all the offline studying, the thinking you do outside of the platform, and whether you actually understood the material versus just going through the motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. For me, that rarely happened this semester. I was just trying to get by. Real engagement would be when you're curious and motivated, not just doing the minimum to pass because you have to.
Q24: It wouldn't show that I was bored out of my mind most of the time, or that I was dealing with stress about money and life stuff. It wouldn't show the studying I did offline or the conversations I had with friends about the material. It also wouldn't show that despite my low activity, I actually understood enough to do okay on exams.
